text:
A book stall run by a woman with ink-stained fingers and the eyes of someone who has read ALL the footnotes. 'Histories,' she says. 'Real ones. Six gold and I don't answer questions about chapter four.'

Nobody affected by "City Book Stall" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that power has commissioned a statue to remember events in the preferred direction.

outcomes.text: The book is heavier than it looks. All the true ones are, she says, counting your coins.

She looks at your purse, then your face, and slides over a slim, water-stained volume. 'Damaged stock. Free. Everyone gets one true book before the world finishes with them.' Chapter four has been torn out. Of course it has.
She stares at you until you leave. Somehow you know three new things about the world anyway, none of them comfortable.

success.text: Four gold, final offer, and she throws in a bookmark shaped like a curse. Deal.

fail.text: 'Haggling? During a WAR?' She raises the price to eight and taps the chapter on sieges. 'You'll be wanting that one soon anyway.' She is not wrong. You pay.
She raises the price to seven, citing 'haggling tax.' You pay it. Chapter four remains unavailable.

text:
A street prophet grabs your sleeve. 'You will die on a TUESDAY,' she hisses. She's selling anti-Tuesday charms, 10 gold each.

Nobody affected by "City Doom Prophet" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that faith has arrived as practical courage; authority has arrived dressed like faith.

outcomes.text: It's a rock with 'NO' painted on it. You feel scammed and yet, somehow, safer.
The rock hums faintly. It might actually be doing something. Unsettling value for money.

She consults her notes, then the sky. 'A WET one,' she adds, gesturing at the rain with sudden confidence. The crowd around her stall doubles on the spot. Weather is her best salesman, and today it is working overtime.
She consults her notes. 'One of them,' she admits. Refreshing honesty. She gives you a discount rock for free.

She opens her mouth to argue, looks at you properly, and stops. 'Oh. OH. Forget Tuesday. You have bigger problems.' She presses a charm into your hand free of charge and backs away making warding signs. Not comforting. Possibly effective.
She screams and sprints away. The crowd applauds. Someone hands you a celebratory pastry.
She smiles. That's the worst possible response. You spend the rest of the day looking over your shoulder.

text:
Fingers, light as regret, brush your coin purse. The pickpocket is already three steps away, whistling.

Nothing about "City Pickpocket" is accidental once you notice that an investor has purchased the problem, removed everything useful, and listed the remainder as growth.

success.text: You catch their wrist mid-whistle. They surrender your purse and, embarrassingly for them, someone else's too.

fail.text: You grab air. They grab your purse, feel the weight of it, and audibly stagger. The escape becomes a lopsided waddle, but the crowd closes behind them anyway. Carrying that much coin was, in retrospect, an advertisement.
You grab air. They grab your purse. The whistling fades into the crowd like an insult.

outcomes.text: 'STOP, THIEF!' you shout. Three citizens look at the pickpocket. Two guards look at YOU, then at a poster, then back. You leave before the comparison completes, purse written off as a professional expense.
The whole street tackles them out of civic enthusiasm. Your gold is returned, slightly dented.
Three other pickpockets use the distraction. Somewhere, a guild quota is met.
You nod. They nod. They keep 5 gold as a 'demonstration fee' and return the rest with a business card.

text:
A guard blocks your path. 'Walking tax,' he says, inventing it. 'Five gold.' His halberd suggests this is not a negotiation.

People remember "City Guard Shakedown" differently, but the material fact remains: authority has mistaken being obeyed for being correct, a common occupational injury.

success.text: You invent subsection 12-B, 'Exemptions for the Visibly Broke.' He writes it down, defeated by bureaucracy.

fail.text: 'War levy,' he corrects, upgrading the extortion with patriotism. Citing tax code during wartime turns out to carry its own surcharge. You pay ten and receive a receipt stamped with a boot.
He invents a fine for 'citing.' Now you owe seven.

outcomes.text: He recognizes you mid-shakedown and turns the extortion into an escort. 'Can't have YOU walking unaccompanied.' No tax. A salute, even.
He takes your coin, then squints. Then unrolls a poster. 'Well, well.' The walking tax becomes a 'fugitive processing fee,' and processing hurts.
He pockets it and waves you through. At least the corruption is efficient here.

success.text: You vanish into an alley. The guard doesn't even give chase; armor is heavy and the day is long.

fail.text: You run directly into a second guard. There's a fee for that, too.

outcomes.text: He looks at the bottle, then down the long gray corridor of his remaining shift. The halberd lowers. 'Tax paid.' You part as the closest thing this city has to friends.
'Bribing a guard? With ONE ale?' He is not offended by the bribe. He is offended by the amount. He takes the ale AND the five gold, as a tutorial.

text:
A vendor's stall displays gleaming junk: 'AUTHENTIC SKY-DEBRIS. FELL OFF A STAR-WAGON. NO REFUNDS.' One piece is blinking.

The official lesson of "City Spaceship Parts" is complicated; the useful lesson is that the machine can provide abundance, but its license agreement remains emotionally committed to scarcity.

outcomes.text: You turn the blinking piece over with a shipowner's eye. It is a bilge sensor. A GOOD bilge sensor. The vendor has no idea what he is sitting on, and you pay his silly price for a part worth triple to anyone with a hull.
It's a 'credit chip,' whatever that is. The vendor swears it's money somewhere. It does feel like money.
It blinks faster in your hand, then stops forever. You have purchased a dead rectangle.
It chirps 'RECALIBRATED' and zaps your brain pleasantly. You suddenly understand fractions.

choices.text: Ask where exactly it fell from

outcomes.text: The vendor points straight up into the falling snow and maintains eye contact while flakes settle on the merchandise. 'Same place as that.' The logic is unassailable. You buy nothing and admire everything.
The vendor points straight up and maintains eye contact. Technically unfalsifiable. You respect it.

choices.text: Report the stall to a guard

outcomes.text: The guard is the vendor's cousin. Everyone is disappointed in you, including, somehow, the merchandise.

text:
A small child in absurdly expensive clothes tugs your sleeve. 'I am LOST,' they announce, with the confidence of someone who has never once faced a consequence.

Ask who pays for "City Lost Noble Child" and the answer begins here: the world is terrible at rewarding decency and strangely dependent upon it.

success.text: You carry the child the last three streets under your coat, out of the rain. The steward watches this from the gate. The reward arrives with a hot meal attached, plus the first sincere thank-you this manor has produced in a generation.
The manor guards initially arrest you, then the child vouches: 'This one is MY SERVANT.' The reward is generous. The title is not.

fail.text: The child directs you, turn by turn, to a candy stall, a puppet show, and a duck pond. By the time you find the manor, the reward has been spent on the journey. By the child.

choices.text: Deliver them to the city guard

outcomes.text: The guard sergeant recognizes the child, goes pale, and thanks you for making it someone else's problem. That's worth a small finder's fee from the guard's own purse.

You almost walk away. Then you remember what is loose in this city, and that small rich lungs are still small lungs. You point the child to the guard post and watch until the door closes behind them. Not your circus. Your conscience anyway.
The child watches you go, then declares, to the entire street, that you are their FAVORITE. You've never felt worse about a decision that cost nothing.

text:
The smithy of {site} rings with hammer-song. The smith looks up, appraises your gear with open pity, and gestures at the racks.

Ask who pays for "City Smith Shop" and the answer begins here: a supervisor has arrived to explain the task to everyone who has performed it for twenty years.

outcomes.text: The racks are half empty and every price has doubled; the army bought everything with an edge. 'War rates,' the smith shrugs, not proud of it. 'Steel goes where the dying is.'
The smith wipes their hands and starts the pitch.

success.text: The smith glows brighter than the forge. Word of your good taste spreads.

fail.text: You compliment the one piece their rival made. The temperature drops. The prices do not.

choices.text: Just warm your hands and leave

outcomes.text: Snow melts off your shoulders in the forge-light. The smith says nothing, hooks a stool closer to the heat with one foot, and lets you sit until feeling returns to your hands. Some kindnesses are load-bearing.
The forge warms you to the bones. The smith nods. Some transactions are free.

text:
A general store, shelves packed floor to ceiling. The sign says 'EVERYTHING MUST GO', and has said so, by the fading, for decades.

Nobody affected by "City General Store" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that a financier calls the missing roof an efficiency and the rain a subscription service.

outcomes.text: The winter stock is out: mittens, lamp oil, rope, and a bin labeled 'SLEDS (BROKEN (FIXABLE (PROBABLY))).' The shopkeeper looks meaningfully at your coat, then at the mitten shelf, then back at your coat.
The shopkeeper emerges from behind a pyramid of jars, delighted to have a customer who isn't a cat.
'Me,' says the shopkeeper, with the eyes of someone forty years into a going-out-of-business sale. You buy nothing but learn much.

choices.text: Offer to repaint the fading sign

outcomes.text: An hour on a ladder with borrowed paint, and EVERYTHING MUST GO stands proud again in confident letters. The shopkeeper is moved nearly to retirement and pays you from a till drawer labeled 'miracles.'
The ladder, it turns out, was also clearance stock. You, the paint, and the letter G descend together.

text:
A recruiter at a folding table, banner overhead, quill ready. 'Sign here and be part of something. Benefits include belonging, a sash, and enemies you didn't choose.'

A plaque may eventually misdescribe "City Faction Recruiter". For now, the useful truth is that the army has requisitioned common sense for ceremonial use.

outcomes.text: The recruiter finds your name already in the ledger of Notable Persons and skips 'member' entirely. You are inducted as an Honorary Something. The sash is nicer. The dues are waived. The meetings, tragically, are not.
You're in. There's a stipend, a sash, and a meeting schedule you will never fully escape.
The fine print includes dues. You've paid to join something you don't understand, which the recruiter calls 'tradition.'

success.text: You dismantle the pitch so thoroughly the recruiter takes the afternoon off to think. Onlookers applaud. One tips you.

fail.text: You lose a debate to a pamphlet. The pamphlet is well-written. You buy the pamphlet.

outcomes.text: You reach for a pamphlet and the recruiter murmurs, without looking up: 'We don't check posters. That is rather the point of us.' It is the most tempting pitch you have heard all year. You keep the pamphlet. You keep walking. Mostly.
The pamphlet is 90% sash diagrams. You feel you understand the organization completely now.

text:
The city fountain. Locals swear it grants wishes. The bottom glitters with the copper of a thousand optimists.

A plaque may eventually misdescribe "City Fountain Wish". For now, the useful truth is that the world is terrible at rewarding decency and strangely dependent upon it.

outcomes.text: Your coin sinks, pauses, and floats back up. The fountain refuses service. Somewhere below, something makes a sound like a lid closing. Even wishes have terms and conditions, and your account is flagged.
Nothing happens, but the plink is satisfying and hope is technically renewable.
The fountain gurgles approvingly. You feel measurably luckier, which should be impossible, and yet.
The fountain spits your coin back with interest. Do not question the fountain's accounting.

choices.text: Wade in and collect the coins

success.text: The snow has emptied the plaza and numbed the water. Nobody watches your harvest but the statues, who have seen worse. Your fingers stop cooperating around coin thirty, which is where an honest person would have stopped anyway.
A swift, shameless harvest. You leave wet, richer, and cursed only by onlookers, which doesn't count.

fail.text: The guard was watching. The fine is itemized: theft of wishes, per wish.

outcomes.text: It's fountain water. It tastes of copper and other people's dreams. You feel briefly connected to everything, then mostly to your stomach.

choices.text: Offer the fountain your lucky rock

outcomes.text: The rock sinks with a plunk of unusual finality. The water goes still. Then every coin at the bottom shifts one inch toward you, which you elect to read as a blessing rather than a warning.
The fountain considers your offering and spits it back, hard. Not every gift is accepted; not every rock is as lucky as advertised. The bruise will be described, later, as 'mystical.'

text:
A street magician is doing card tricks. The cards are floating. Actual levitation for copper tips, while three blocks away the guild sells the same magic for a fortune.

Nothing about "City Street Magician" is accidental once you notice that the work is necessary, the workers are disposable, and the contradiction has begun making noise.

success.text: Threads of enchanted spider-silk. You catch the magician's eye; they wink and cut you in for keeping quiet.

fail.text: You stare so hard you volunteer yourself by accident. Your coin purse is now an egg. The crowd loves it. The magician cannot change it back.

outcomes.text: You tip two of your last coins. The magician notices exactly what that costs you, makes them vanish, and returns them behind your ear with a wink. 'Trade rule: never rob the poor. No margins in it.'
The magician makes your tip vanish, then makes a rose appear behind your ear. Net loss, gross delight.

You heckle, and the crowd gasps: a celebrity feud! The magician, no fool, challenges you to a duel of showmanship on the spot and splits the doubled take with you afterward. Everyone was a plant. It was beautiful.
The magician makes your eyebrows swap places for an hour. The crowd sides with the magician. So do your eyebrows.
Your heckle is so precise the magician hires you as a plant. Two shows later you've earned honest dishonest wages.

text:
The public bathhouse: steam, echoes, and the total collapse of social rank. Somewhere in the fog a voice holds court: "{rumor}"

Officials classify "City Bathhouse" as routine, although a life can be saved cheaply, creating a serious problem for the people charging more.

outcomes.text: Half the pools are roped off and the attendant swears the steam 'boils the miasma out.' Medically dubious, spiritually unimpeachable. You emerge scalded pink and feeling, for one evening, immortal.

Winter has made the bathhouse the most crowded building in the city. You soak shoulder to shoulder with strangers, every rank and grudge dissolved at temperature. Leaving is the hardest thing you do all week.
Two hours of heat, gossip, and mineral water. You emerge pruned, informed, and at peace.

choices.text: Correct the storyteller from inside the fog

outcomes.text: An anonymous debate erupts across the steam. You win. Someone unseen slides you a respect pastry.
The fog turns hostile. You are asked to leave by consensus splash.

choices.text: Check the changing room for unattended purses

success.text: A professional sweep. You even leave the sentimental lockets, because you're a thief, not a monster.

fail.text: The attendant was a retired adventurer. The towel-whip is a martial art here.

choices.text: Sell your incense to the attendant

outcomes.text: The attendant sniffs it and her eyes widen. 'Real cedar? The patrons will think we've raised our standards.' You are paid on the spot and comped a soak while your former property perfumes the fog.
The attendant burns a sample. Somewhere in the fog, someone is violently allergic. The incense is returned. So are you, to the street.

text:
A crowd has gathered in the square. On the scaffold, a man is being sentenced for 'aggravated mime-craft.' The crowd's mood is somewhere between festival and refund.

Power calls "City Execution Crowd" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: authority has mistaken being obeyed for being correct, a common occupational injury.

success.text: You step forward and the crowd recognizes you before you speak. The magistrate, sensing which way the ballads will be written, commutes the sentence mid-gasp. The mime performs 'gratitude' so hard it becomes briefly visible.
Your speech about proportionality moves the magistrate to tears and the sentence to community theater. The mime thanks you, silently but at length.

fail.text: The crowd boos you in unison. The mime, professionally, mimes booing you as well. Even the condemned have standards.

success.text: Executions are a pickpocket's harvest festival. You tithe yourself generously from six pockets.

fail.text: Your target was the executioner, off-duty. There's a fine. He deducts it directly, by handshake.

outcomes.text: The snow has thinned the crowd to the truly committed, which is its own verdict on everyone still standing there. You leave your bootprints pointing away from the scaffold and think better of them than most of your tracks.
You walk away from the roar of the square. Somewhere behind you, an invisible box gets its final performance. Your conscience travels light today.

text:
A currency exchange run by a woman with an abacus, a sky-metal calculator, and visible contempt for both. The board lists today's rate for 'star money' in chalk, already crossed out twice.

Behind the business of "City Credit Exchange" sits an older truth: a merchant has found a way to own the risk while renting it back to everyone in danger.

outcomes.text: She spots the salt on your boots and the registry tag on your keys. 'Shipowner rate.' She pays out fair and throws in a chalk list of which ports still honor sky money, 'because captains gossip, and gossip is advertising.'
She counts out coins with the weariness of someone who's seen currencies die. Fair rate, all things considered.
The rate improved while you stood there. She honors the chalk, cursing softly. Windfall.
'War discount,' she says, sliding extra chips across. 'Everyone is dumping sky money for steel and bread. Their panic, your portfolio.' The abacus clicks in what might be approval.
'Investing in the sky economy,' she says, deadpan, sliding the blinking chips across. 'Bold.'

success.text: You and she talk monetary theory for an hour. Her conclusion: 'turnips, eventually.' You leave smarter and vaguely alarmed.

fail.text: She answers entirely in exchange-rate jargon. You nod along and accidentally agree to a small transaction fee for the lesson.
